questions.text: I prefer to set the ground rules before any group activity begins.
I find that I am generally unaware of the emotional undercurrents in my relationships.
I'm comfortable with ambiguity about where a project stands as long as progress feels real.
I rarely know what I'll be doing tomorrow until tomorrow comes.
I feel that it is my responsibility to point the way when people are lost.
I find that my emotional responses ramp up faster than I can consciously process what's happening.
I sense the invisible threads of obligation and reciprocity in social groups.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is what prevents me from becoming complacent about ethics.
I rehearse difficult conversations in my mind before having them.
I rarely notice when a person carefully positions a negative opinion as a question to soften the delivery.
I tend to describe my feelings in precise, measured terms rather than superlatives.
I find that choosing my own comfort over shared inconvenience is usually the right call.
I detect when a friend group has a designated peacekeeper who steps in at predictable moments.
I find that self-focus is what allows me to be consistent and reliable.
I feel that the pull to respond when I become aware of suffering is among the most trustworthy moral signals I experience.
I find that I am able to process even the most distressing accounts of human suffering without my emotional stability being compromised.
I tend to feel settled and grounded as my default state.
I would forgo a hobby I love if the time could be better spent helping my community.
I find that my instinct when I hear about someone's problem is to offer a concrete solution, not just sympathy.
I want to be part of shaping how a project unfolds, alongside others.
I believe that my emotional steadiness is a constitutional trait, not something I have to work at.
I believe that the ability to engage with suffering on a purely intellectual level is an asset in most professional contexts.
I do not usually detect when a question is rhetorical versus when someone actually wants an answer.
I evaluate new opportunities against my existing priorities before saying yes.
I notice that my breathing changes dramatically when I'm emotionally activated.
I tend to put my phone away and give my full presence to whoever is in front of me.
I believe that the drive to achieve personal goals is one of the healthiest motivations.
I am inclined to keep options open until the group has weighed in.
I often find that I help the group define what success looks like together.
Witnessing someone else in pain produces an almost identical sensation in my own body.
I start writing and let the structure emerge from the material itself.
I sense when a farewell is warm on the surface but carries relief underneath.
I write down open questions and revisit them systematically until they're resolved.
I find that approaching human problems with structured analytical thinking is my most reliable method for producing outcomes that are both effective and just.
I find it natural to tell others what I expect from them.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is a moral demand whose urgency does not diminish with time, distance, or the number of times I have encountered it.
